Role: QA Engineer
Send me your resume to my E- sukesh@nebulapartners.com 

Description:
Validate products through the use of systematic tests to develop, apply, and maintain quality standards.
Analyze logs and failures that may span Hardware (HW), Software (SW), and Firmware (FW) and propose debug and mitigation strategies.
Contribute to the continuous improvement of testing strategies, tools, and processes to drive innovation and quality.
Work with AI tools to facilitate the automation process.
Adding new automation to improve code coverage and overall product quality; identifying gaps and contributing to near-100% automation goals.
Developing OS/System-Level Automation and Test Automation software/scripting/etc.
Develop, modify, and execute test plans.
Maintain documentation of test results to assist in debugging and modification of GPUs.
Provide root cause and corrective action plans in a timely manner and write clear and complete reports detailing steps taken and findings.

Your last CPO owned cost reduction.

Your next one needs to own resilience, AI adoption, supplier ecosystems, and revenue generation.

Same title. Completely different job.

The CPO role has fundamentally shifted in the last few years. Tariff volatility, nearshoring decisions, AI vendor contracts, Scope 3 compliance, supplier partnerships that open commercial doors, these all sit with procurement now. Whether procurement is ready for them or not.

But most hiring processes haven't caught up. Companies are still screening for category management experience and savings track records. Both matter. Neither is enough anymore.

The result is a hire that looks right on paper and struggles in the role. Not because they're not good. Because the job they were hired to do and the job that actually exists are two different things.

I've seen this play out more than once this year already. The candidate was strong. The brief was outdated. And six months in, everyone's frustrated.
Getting this hire right starts before the search does. It starts with an honest conversation about what the business actually needs from procurement in 2026, not what it needed when the last person was hired.

When did your organization last update what it actually expects from its procurement leadership?

🌟NOW HIRING: Responsabil conformitate și colectare junior (teren)🌟

📣 Pentru clientul nostru, companie activă în domeniul protecției drepturilor de autor, căutăm un Responsabil conformitate și colectare junior (teren). Suntem în căutarea unei persoane aflate la început de drum, cu inițiativă, orientată către dezvoltare și învățare continuă. Mediul de lucru este dinamic, stabil și bine organizat, oferind perspective reale de creștere și dezvoltare profesională.

👉 Rolul postului constă în identificarea în teren a operatorilor economici din zona alocată, încheierea autorizațiilor necesare, colectarea remunerațiilor aferente și gestionarea relației cu utilizatorii, inclusiv urmărirea sumelor restante și informarea acestora privind obligațiile legale.

📍 Locație: Ialomița, Călărași, Ilfov

🔹 Responsabilități principale:
-> Identificarea în teren a operatorilor economici din zona alocată
-> Încheierea autorizațiilor în numele organizației
-> Colectarea remunerațiilor aferente autorizațiilor
-> Urmărirea și solicitarea plăților restante
-> Comunicarea permanentă cu utilizatorii gestionați
-> Informarea acestora privind obligațiile legale aplicabile

Ce oferim:
✔ Contract de muncă full-time
✔ Salariu fix + bonus lunar de performanță în funcție de încasări
✔ Tichete de masă, telefon, tabletă și decont carburant
✔ Mediu de lucru dinamic și oportunități de dezvoltare profesională

💡 Dacă te afli la început de drum și îți dorești un rol cu impact, în care să înveți, să te dezvolți și să ai activitate dinamică de teren, acest rol ar putea fi potrivit pentru tine. 

📥 Dacă acest tip de rol ți-a atras atenția și te consideri potrivit, mi-ar face plăcere să te cunoaștem și să discutăm mai multe detalii. Ca prim pas în procesul de recrutare, te rugăm să ne trimiți un CV detaliat la adresa recrutare1@abchumancapital.ro.

Every founder is asking the same question:

Which GTM motion actually prints pipeline in 2026?

I've watched teams run outbound, PLG, ABM, and enterprise sales back-to-back for 18 months.

Some compound. Some work in weeks. Some drain 12 months of runway.

Here's the honest ranking of the 6 GTM models that matter in 2026.

1. Outbound-Led (AI-Enriched).

Pipeline in weeks, not quarters. Waterfall enrichment across 150+ data providers, AI SDRs on first-touch, signal plays on funding and hiring. Cheapest motion to start.
Companies: Clay, Apollo, 11x, Gong, Outreach.

2. Product-Led Growth.

The product is the sales rep. OpenView data shows PLG companies grow 50% vs a 21% SaaS mean. Cracks when enterprise deals need a sales layer.
Companies: Figma, Slack, Notion, Calendly, Canva.

3. Account-Based Marketing.

Markets of one. Intent data from 6sense and Bombora times the plays. 171% ACV lift vs non-ABM accounts. $8K to $30K per month stack.
Companies: 6sense, Demandbase, Bombora, Snowflake.

4. Marketing-Led (Inbound).

Content and SEO pull buyers in. 62% lower CPL than outbound. Compounds for years. But AI overviews are eating organic clicks faster than anyone wants to admit.
Companies: HubSpot, Ahrefs, Semrush, Webflow, Zapier.

5. Sales-Led (Enterprise).

AEs, MEDDIC, 6 to 18 month cycles. Highest contract values. Most predictable math. AE OTE starts at $250K. Wrong motion to pick pre-Series A.
Companies: Salesforce, SAP, ZoomInfo, Clari, Chorus.

6. Community-Led Growth.

Users become the pipeline. Notion runs on 95% organic traffic. Hardest to attribute, deepest moat. Slowest to ROI.
Companies: GitHub, Atlassian, Duolingo, Common Room.

Most founders pick the wrong one for their stage.

The right order in 2026: start with outbound-led, layer PLG when the product can stand alone, add sales-led when ACV clears $50K.

Every other sequence costs runway.

Most people arguing about AI and jobs are both right - and both missing half the story.

𝗥𝗼𝗹𝗲𝘀 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗲𝘃𝗼𝗹𝘃𝗶𝗻𝗴, 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗱𝗶𝘀𝗮𝗽𝗽𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴.

The underwriter who reviewed 40 contracts a week now reviews 200 with AI assist. The job didn't die. It leveled up.

And the static market argument is underrated: demand expands when cost drops. That's not theory - that's what's already happening.

𝗕𝘂𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗻𝘀𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗼𝗱 𝗶𝘀 𝗯𝗿𝘂𝘁𝗮𝗹.

→ Retraining takes 12–18 months
↳ Upskilling budgets get cut first
→ Entry-level hiring is already slowing in AI-exposed roles

The market isn't static. But 𝑝𝑒𝑜𝑝𝑙𝑒 are - on their own timelines.

The long-run story is optimistic. The medium-run story has real casualties.

If you're leading a team in an AI-exposed function, where are you in that transition window?

After 2 years building production AI systems, a pattern:

The cost of an LLM app rarely comes from the model.
It comes from the invisible stack around it.

This week I'll share what I've actually shipped:

+ Cut LLM infrastructure cost 3× by tracing the pipeline with LangFuse and spotting that a "free" model was the biggest spender

+ Built a meta-skill that turned a 4-6 day feature cycle into 3 hours, and reduced review loops from 6 to 2

+ Built full-stack observability for LLM workflows (LangFuse + Prometheus + Loki, unified via OpenTelemetry) — per-request token, latency, cost tracking

+ Shipped production agentic workflows on LangGraph with state, checkpoints, tool calling, RAG with Sentence Transformers

+ Migrated a NestJS backend to FastAPI to align with the LLM ecosystem, without breaking API contracts

Five years as a backend engineer before that — DDD, event-driven systems, SAGA, idempotency at scale. The reliability instincts didn't go anywhere.

Now looking for a remote team building serious AI products.
Founders, CTOs, eng leads — my DMs are open.

The Reality of Staffing in 2026: What Recruiters Must Know

The staffing industry has changed—fast. Today, recruiters aren’t just filling roles, they’re navigating a tech-driven, candidate-first market.
Here’s what’s really happening 👇

1. Stability with Pressure
The market is stabilizing, but expectations are higher—clients want faster, more accurate hiring. Quality beats volume.

2. AI is Changing the Game
AI won’t replace recruiters—but it will replace outdated recruiting habits. Speed + human judgment is the winning combo.

3. Skills Over Degrees
Hiring is now about real skills, not just qualifications. Recruiters must assess capability, not just keywords.

4. Rise of Contract Staffing
Flexibility is key. Contract roles are growing as companies focus on cost control and project-based hiring.

5. Candidate Expectations Are Higher
Speed, transparency, and communication matter more than ever. Recruiters are now brand ambassadors.

6. New Talent Pools
Opportunities are expanding into non-traditional and untapped markets—smart recruiters are exploring them.

Bottom Line:
The industry isn’t shrinking—it’s evolving.

Recruiters who think strategically, use tech smartly, and build strong relationships will stay ahead.

💬 What changes are you seeing in your market?

Most founders think going global means setting up a legal entity first.

That assumption quietly kills expansion timelines — and burns capital before you've made a single hire abroad.

Here's what entity setup actually costs you: 
Legal structuring fees. 
Local registered agents. 
Annual compliance filings.
Local accounting and audit obligations. 
Minimum capital requirements in some jurisdictions. 
And 4–6 months of calendar time, just to get the right to hire.

For a startup validating a new market or an enterprise testing headcount in a region—that's an unreasonable bet to make upfront.

There's a category of infrastructure built exactly for this. It's called Employer of Record (EOR) — and most teams still underuse it.

With Asanify AI's EOR solution, you hire in 50+ countries without touching an entity. 

We become the legal employer on record—handling local contracts, payroll disbursement, tax withholding, statutory benefits, and compliance monitoring — end to end, on Day 1.

What makes Asanify AI different isn't just the geography coverage. It's the AI layer underneath:
— Contracts auto-generated to local labor law standards 
— Payroll processed with AI-led compliance checks, not manual review 
— Real-time alerts when statutory obligations shift in your target country 
— A single dashboard for a globally distributed workforce

If you're a CHRO rethinking global hiring strategy, a founder about to test a new market, or an expansion lead weighing "entity vs. EOR" — the math has changed.

The old model: spend ₹30–80L, wait 6 months, then hire. 
The Asanify AI model: hire first. Expand intelligently. Build the entity only when the market earns it.

Global growth shouldn't be gated by legal infrastructure.

👉 Explore Asanify AI EOR services in the comments.

The U.S. staffing market isn’t slowing down; it’s evolving.

In 2026, companies are still hiring, but with tighter filters, longer approvals, and higher expectations. At the same time, candidates are more selective, prioritizing stability over speed.

What’s changing:
Hiring is now skill-specific, not volume-driven
More interview rounds and slower decisions
Contract roles face higher scrutiny from candidates
AI supports hiring, but human judgment still leads

Reality check: More applicants do not mean faster hiring. Conversion rates are tighter than ever.

What works now: Clear role alignment, transparent communication, and quality over quantity in hiring pipelines.

The takeaway: This is not a slowdown, it’s a recalibration. The teams that adapt will outperform.

We are hiring a **SaaS Contracting Attorney – AI Fellowship** for **micro1 job portal**, a global platform that connects legal and domain experts with advanced AI training, legal reasoning systems, and high-value data annotation projects. This role is designed for experienced legal professionals specializing in SaaS contracts, negotiation, and contract lifecycle management who can contribute to building AI systems with strong legal intelligence capabilities.

In this role, you will work on reviewing SaaS agreements, analyzing contract clauses, performing redlining tasks, and structuring legal datasets for AI training. Your contributions will directly improve AI systems used in legal tech, contract automation, compliance intelligence, and enterprise risk assessment. You will help ensure AI models understand legal language, obligations, liabilities, and negotiation patterns with high accuracy.

* Review and analyze **SaaS contracts and legal agreements**
* Perform **contract redlining and clause structuring** for AI datasets
* Support **contract negotiation analysis and optimization**
* Identify legal risks, obligations, and compliance issues in contracts
* Annotate legal documents for AI training in legal reasoning systems
* Contribute to structured datasets for contract intelligence models
* Validate legal content accuracy using legal frameworks and references
* Assist in improving AI understanding of enterprise SaaS legal workflows

### Required Skills

* Strong expertise in **SaaS contract review and legal analysis**
* Experience in **contract negotiation and redlining**
* Understanding of commercial contracts, licensing, and enterprise agreements
* Strong legal reasoning and analytical skills
* Experience in legal documentation or corporate law (preferred)
* Ability to structure complex legal text for AI training datasets

### Compensation

* Pay: **$80 – $105 per hour**

This opportunity at **micro1** contributes to advancing AI in legal technology by integrating expert-level SaaS contract knowledge into structured datasets that power next-generation legal intelligence and contract automation systems.

l have been working on an AI agent for SEO/GEO - one that can create content, learn from your judgement over time about brand, and keep improving the level of output. Something like OpenClaw for SEO/GEO. 

Reddit Marketing is also going on and going well but given the pushback by Reddit on using technology on the platform - it is hard to scale without humans. Which makes sense given it's the most "human place on the internet". And I do not want to fight the platform on trying to automate it because of the risk. One update and everything can go to zero. So, it's better to do it organically.  I will be lying if I said I did not try to do it. But, every week I had to find new solutions and it's pointless.

The tricky part is also the clients. Hardly 10% of the clients I speak with agree to do it organically. Most do not even believe me when I say I do it organically as a human sitting and engaging. But, it was 0% when I started 8-9 months back so no complaints. It took some time to find people who were interested in doing Reddit organically. A lot of people just trusted me so thank you so much. Grateful to all of them. 

Everyone thinks Reddit is full of bots. It is, but nobody actually cares about them - neither the users nor the LLMs. Also, Reddit discontinued their API very recently in November 2025 (due to obvious reasons, bots) so bots have largely decreased now. 

Going forward, I am not sure if I want to scale humans on the Reddit marketing side. Services business is fun but I don't want something >80% service led. I definitely want to move up the ladder with bigger clients & larger contracts so I can just keep doing this (without hiring & making more money) while also reinvesting money into building some stuff. Somehow merge both the things I do - Reddit and GEO atleast to maybe 70-80% automation if not 100%. Let's see.

Nonetheless, if you or someone you know is looking for Reddit Marketing, please do reach out :)

RPO and MSP aren't going anywhere anytime soon, regardless of AI disruption. The industry, like most others, will be redefined, and it will be an exciting time of transformation for those companies that are willing to evolve.

For the last 10 years or so, both models have competed largely on cost and compliance, markup compression, headcount efficiency, and process consistency. This era is about to end, and the next ten years will separate two types of providers...

Those still selling on effort, headcount, and administration against those who sell intelligence, architecture, and outcomes.

RPO moves from managing requisitions to designing talent operating models: skills-based hiring, internal mobility, workforce planning, and employer branding. Think less outsourced recruiter, more strategic partner to CHROs and the leader of TA.

MSP moves from policing vendor markups to governing enterprise workforce strategy: classification risk, cross-border compliance, services procurement visibility, variable capacity design. Think less vendor program admin, more workforce intelligence function.

Many firms are converging both now and have been for some time. Large enterprise customers increasingly want one answer to the same question: what's the best way to access the capability they need to hire: contract, SOW, redeploy, or automate?

The firms that win this war on talent can bridge HR and procurement, connect data across all systems, and help clients answer that question. Those who lose out are the ones still competing on who can shave another few dollars off the markup.

This is a direction of travel, not a true description of where today's market is. Most customers, especially across the mid-market, still primarily select RPO and MSP on cost alone. The segment that wants genuine workforce intelligence and talent architecture does exist, but it isn't quite the whole market just yet.

The transition is also harder to execute than it is to describe. "Become a strategic partner to the CHRO" sounds clean on paper and fairly straightforward, but building the advisory talent, the proprietary data, and the credibility to actually deliver it is a different thing entirely. Most providers simply aren't there yet.

AI in recruiting, while real and becoming more advanced each day, is still too early to prove ROI at scale. Enterprise procurement moves slowly, and the transition to fully automating at scale will be uneven, with many teething issues along the way.

The future isn't bigger teams doing more transactions...it's fewer people doing more valuable work on top of automation. That future arrives unevenly, and the gap between aspiration and execution in this industry has always been wide.

Where do you see RPO and MSP heading?

IS YOUR HR DEPARTMENT USING AI FOR HIRING, PERFORMANCE REVIEWS, OR TASK ALLOCATION?

From 2 August 2026, many of these systems will be legally classified as "high-risk" under the EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689). This is NOT a future problem. The countdown has already started.

WHAT QUALIFIES AS "HIGH-RISK" IN EMPLOYMENT?
Annex III of the AI Act explicitly lists AI systems used for:
- Recruitment and selection (CV screening, candidate ranking)
- Promotion and termination decisions
- Task allocation based on individual behaviour or personal traits
- Monitoring and evaluating performance

WHY IT MATTERS FOR MULTINATIONALS OPERATING IN THE EU:
If your HR tech stack includes these capabilities, you will be legally required to: conduct a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment; guarantee human oversight on every employment decision; ensure transparency with candidates and employees; register the system in the EU database; maintain detailed technical documentation; comply with GDPR AND the AI Act simultaneously.

THE RISK: Non-compliance fines up to EUR 15 million or 3% of global turnover, whichever is higher. Plus reputational damage and potential employment litigation.

OUR ADVICE FOR INTERNATIONAL EMPLOYERS:
1. Map every AI tool currently used in your HR processes
2. Classify each according to Annex III
3. Request AI Act compliance documentation from your vendors
4. Update your employment contracts and privacy notices
5. Train HR and managers on mandatory human oversight

AdvaLux assists multinationals in navigating the intersection between EU AI Act, GDPR and Italian employment law. Cross-border compliance is our specialty.

Only 2.8% of staffing firms show up when AI answers a buyer's question.

I ran a study on 646 firms to figure out what those 18 have that the other 628 don't.

First, the enterprise ceiling. Five brands ate up 40% of every mention. 
Robert Half. TEKsystems. Insight Global. Kelly Services. Aerotek.

Then the 18 others that did show up all had 3 things in common.

1. They write for employers. Not candidates. Not job seekers. The person who signs the contract.

2. They answer specific buyer questions. How to evaluate a staffing partner. When to use contract vs. direct hire. Healthcare staffing ratios. Not "5 tips for a great company culture."

3. They publish their own data. Salary benchmarks. Turnover reports. Market analysis their competitors can't pull out of thin air.

If a hiring manager pulled up 40 AI queries about your niche tomorrow, how many answers would include your name?

The AI Frontier: Redefining IT Asset Management
​As digital infrastructure scales, managing everything from hardware silicon to LLM tokens has grown exponentially complex. Organizations are moving beyond simple tracking toward AI-driven intelligence.
​By shifting to an advanced AI framework, IT Managers gain 30,000-foot visibility paired with granular, actionable insights across the tech stack.

​The 8 Pillars of AI-Powered Intelligence

​User Intelligence: Links assets to POs and budgets while tracking CPU utilization vs. actual workload and AI consumption costs.

​Hardware Intelligence: Maps the full lifecycle of global fleets. AI flags telemetry anomalies to enable proactive maintenance before issues arise.

​Software & SaaS Intelligence: Centralizes contracts with total spend visibility and proactive renewal alerts (30/60/90 days).

​Procurement & Inventory: Shifts from reactive to Predictive Procurement by forecasting regional unit needs based on hiring trends and device health.

​Storage & Cloud: Monitors "cost-drift" by identifying untagged resources and moving "cold data" to lower-cost tiers like S3 or Blob.

​Compute & AI: Optimizes GPU spend by measuring idle time and identifying opportunities to swap expensive LLMs for specialized, cost-effective models.

​DLs & Groups Intelligence: Organizes communication infrastructure by cost center and traces ownership of "orphaned" assets.

​Physical Server Intelligence: Optimizes on-premise hardware through utilization analytics, predictive failure forecasting, and energy efficiency monitoring.

​The Impact

​Cost Optimization: Eliminates waste by highlighting underutilized contracts.

​AI ROI: Measures the adoption of tools (Gemini, Copilot) against actual operational support costs.

​AI ensures every dollar spent on hardware and cloud works as hard as the teams they support.

Why good CMOs are so hard to find in game companies — and what that actually costs you.

I've helped hire, evaluate, or replace CMOs across a lot of studios. The pattern is consistent enough that I want to write it out.

─────────────────────────
The role most studios think they're hiring for

"We need someone to run marketing." Usually that means: manage the UA team, own the creative pipeline, report on ROAS.

That's a media buyer with a title upgrade. That's not a CMO.

─────────────────────────
What a CMO in a game company actually needs to do

A real CMO in a global game studio holds four things simultaneously:

1. Brand architecture — what does this game mean to the player? Not what does it say in the app store description, but what emotional contract does it make and keep?

2. Market intelligence — not dashboards, but the ability to synthesize signals from player behavior, competitive movement, and cultural context into decisions the product team can act on

3. Growth systems — the CMO owns the model that connects acquisition cost to LTV to payback period. If they can't build that model themselves, they're going to be dependent on whoever can, and that creates organizational blind spots

4. Cross-functional authority — the CMO who only controls the marketing budget controls very little. The decisions that actually affect marketing outcomes live in product, in monetization, in CS. A CMO without influence in those rooms is a very expensive communications manager.

─────────────────────────
Why this person is rare

Each of these four capabilities is a full career track. Finding someone who's developed all four — and can hold them simultaneously under the pressure of a live game — is genuinely difficult.

The CMOs I've seen succeed in game companies almost always came from the product or operations side, not the agency side. They had to learn marketing. But they already understood the game.

─────────────────────────
What the gap costs

Most studios don't realize their CMO gap is costing them money until they've been losing it for two years. By then, the creative direction has drifted, the UA team has been optimizing toward the wrong signals, and the game's identity in the market is blurry enough that paid acquisition efficiency has been declining for months.

The fix isn't just hiring better. It's being honest about which of the four capabilities you actually need most urgently — and building the team around that answer, rather than hiring one person to carry all four.

*(Based on GGC consulting experience across Sims, Casino, and AI product categories)*

---

为什么好的CMO在游戏公司这么难找——以及这个缺口真正的代价是什么。

大多数工作室以为自己在招"管市场的人"。通常意思是：管UA团队，管创意管线，汇报ROAS。那是个有头衔的媒介买手，不是CMO。

一个真正的游戏公司CMO需要同时掌握四件事：

1. 品牌架构——这款游戏对玩家意味着什么？不是应用商店的描述文案，而是它和玩家之间的情感契约。

2. 市场情报——不是仪表盘，而是把玩家行为信号、竞品动向、文化语境综合成产品团队可以行动的决策。

3. 增长系统——CMO拥有连接获客成本、LTV和回收周期的模型。如果他们自己建不了这个模型，就会依赖别人，产生组织盲点。

4. 跨职能影响力——只控制营销预算的CMO控制的东西很少。真正影响营销结果的决策在产品、变现和客服里。进不了那些房间的CMO是个很贵的传播经理。

这四种能力每一种都是一条完整的职业轨迹。能同时掌握四种、并在一款活跃游戏的压力下运转的人，确实罕见。

我见过的在游戏公司成功的CMO，几乎都来自产品或运营侧，不是来自代理公司侧——他们是后来学的营销，但他们已经懂游戏。

大多数工作室要等亏损两年后才意识到CMO缺口在烧钱。那时候，创意方向已经飘了，UA团队已经在优化错误的信号，游戏在市场上的定位已经模糊到付费获客效率下滑了好几个月。

*(基于GGC在Sims、Casino和AI产品品类的咨询经验)*

Something I've noticed after training 300+ professionals:

The gap isn't ethics. It isn't even regulation knowledge.

It's translation.

Legal reads the regulation and produces policy documents. Engineering 
reads those documents and has no idea what to build differently. 
Compliance sits in between, translating imperfectly in both directions.

And the regulatory pressure is no longer hypothetical.

Colorado's AI Act is active now. NYC's Local Law 144 has been enforcing 
bias audits on hiring AI since 2023. NIST AI RMF is showing up in 
government contracts and enterprise procurement. The SEC and FTC are 
actively enforcing on AI claims. EU AI Act enforcement begins August 
2026 for any company selling into Europe.

That translation gap is costing teams real money right now.

This is the work I've been spending most of my time on. After years of 
research on certified unlearning, fairness under erasure, 
and AI governance (with submissions to NeurIPS and IEEE), I have been 
building an advisory practice focused specifically on this translation 
problem. Under the banner of The 1% AI Cognitive Fund, I now work with 
AI-native startups, enterprise teams, and regulated companies on NIST 
AI RMF implementation, state law compliance, EU AI Act readiness, 
agentic systems evaluation, and responsible AI operations.

The positioning has a name now: AI Governance & Compliance Advisor.

To launch that work publicly, I am running a free lightning lesson on 
Maven that does the translation work explicitly. It's built for 
engineers, not AI ethics theorists.

Saturday, May 2, 10 AM EST. 30 minutes. Free.

What we'll cover:

> The 4-tier risk classification system that underpins NIST AI RMF, 
EU AI Act, Colorado AI Act, NYC Local Law 144, and most emerging 
frameworks

> The 3 core technical artifacts auditors actually inspect, and the 
failure modes that consistently flag audits

> A 10-item checklist you can use Monday morning to verify any AI 
system in your pipeline

You will leave with the mental model, the checklist as a PDF, and the 
AI Governance Readiness Scorecard I use with advisory clients.

Saturday, May 2
10 AM EST / 7 AM PST / 4 PM CEST

Register: https://lnkd.in/dUidg_JK

Built for engineering leaders, ML/AI engineers, and anyone whose AI 
systems face regulatory pressure (which is now, regardless of market). 
No ethics lectures. No governance software pitches. Just practitioner 
work.

Many organizations still use AI like a layer of sprinkles on top of what they are already doing. 

Literally breaking down a process into steps and adding some AI to this. Writing an email with AI. Generating a job description. Or creating a summary of a meeting. Useful? Absolutely. But that is mostly bolt-on AI. 

The real value only starts to emerge when you stop adding AI to individual tasks but instead reconfigure the entire process with AI at its core - 𝗕𝘂𝗶𝗹𝘁-𝗶𝗻 𝗔𝗜. 

Take the hiring process. In many organizations, it consists of dozens of separate steps: approval for the hire, writing the job spec, posting the vacancy online, screening candidates, scheduling interviews, organizing approvals, making an offer, drafting the contract, and arranging onboarding. 

All separate activities, spread across teams, systems, and departments. 

You can use AI to write just the job description. Or you can redesign the entire process around the real job to be done: hiring the right person. 

Then you are no longer thinking in 94 process steps, but in orchestration. Multiple agents working together. One for intake, one for job specs, one for candidate selection and so on. Not as separate tools, but as one connected flow. 

Built-in AIThat is the difference between doing things differently and 𝗱𝗼𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗱𝗶𝗳𝗳𝗲𝗿𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴𝘀. 

We are already moving into the next wave of AI. Increasing productivity is something everyone is working on. Now the challenge is to redesign end-to-end processes so you can get to the real question: 

What does this mean for your operating- and businessmodel? 

Because when work, services and decision-making fundamentally change, what your organization offers will eventually change as well. 

That is why leaders need to look beyond their own functions. Not from today’s process steps, but from the work that truly needs to be done. And they need to help their people move with it, learn, and redesign how work happens. 

That is what built-in AI is about. Read more about the EY.ai Value Blueprints: 

https://lnkd.in/eaTds_4X 

Where do you think your organization stands? 

#AI #AgenticAI #Leadership #Transformation #EYAI #Innovation

🚀 Exploring Real-World Applications of NLP & Chatbots Across Industries
Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Chatbots are transforming the way businesses operate and interact with users. From healthcare to finance, recruitment to legal services—AI-driven solutions are reshaping entire industries.
💡 Chatbots in Healthcare
AI-powered chatbots like Molly, Eva, Ginger, Replika, Florence, and Izzy assist patients by providing end-to-end support—from basic diagnosis guidance to treatment suggestions, medication reminders, and even first aid assistance.
🧠 Mental Health Support
Tools like Wysa and Woebot act as virtual companions, helping users express emotions, manage stress, and improve mental well-being through intelligent conversations.
🏦 Chatbots in Banking
Banks such as Bank of America, JPMorgan Chase, and Wells Fargo leverage chatbots to handle tasks like balance inquiries, loan queries, and account details—allowing human experts to focus on complex issues.
⚖️ Legal AI Assistants
Solutions like DoNotPay, LISA, ROSS Intelligence, and BillyBot are revolutionizing legal services by enabling faster research, contract review, and document automation.
📊 NLP in Brand Sentiment Analysis
Organizations use NLP to analyze customer opinions from social media and public platforms, helping them understand brand perception and respond proactively.
💼 NLP in Recruitment
Semantic search powered by NLP allows companies to efficiently filter resumes and identify the most suitable candidates, reducing hiring time and effort.
📞 Optimizing Call Center Operations
With voice-to-text and intelligent query analysis, NLP helps prioritize customer issues and deliver faster, more personalized support.
⌨️ Everyday NLP Applications
Smart tools like Microsoft SwiftKey enhance typing with predictive text, while Grammarly improves writing quality by correcting grammar, spelling, and tone.
✨ Conclusion
From enhancing customer experience to automating complex workflows, NLP and Chatbots are no longer futuristic concepts—they are essential tools driving innovation across industries.

The International Bar Association Employment and Diversity Law Conference 2026 brings together leading experts from around the world to explore the evolving intersection of artificial intelligence and employment law.
 
As AI continues to reshape the modern workplace, the conference focuses on key legal and practical challenges — from algorithmic hiring and workplace surveillance to data privacy, bias in AI systems, and the impact of automation on labour rights and employment contracts.
 
Across jurisdictions, regulatory developments and compliance requirements are rapidly evolving. The conference provides a platform for cross-border exchange on how employers can navigate this increasingly complex landscape, while also addressing broader topics such as diversity, pay transparency, and global workforce strategies.
 
Our experts Karolin Andréewitch-Wallner (Taylor Wessing Österreich) and Krystian Stanasiuk, LL.M. (Taylor Wessing Poland) will be on site to engage in discussions with international peers.
 
Many thanks to the International Bar Association for organising this important conference!

Where is the EoR ( Employer of Record ) market going?

reesmarx has hired for clients in UK/Europe, Americas and throughout APAC, hiring directly for Technology, Software and Fintech clients and partnering with our EoR partners to hire for many different industries. Due to our close relationship, working with the EoR market, I looked at how it might grow and change in the coming years.

In my view, I think this market space will see:

More use with AI & automation - Their platforms will integrate more AI for payroll, monitoring compliance, and predictive analytics.

Further Human Resources Integration - They will evolve more comprehensive H.R. platforms, utilising tools for onboarding, payroll, benefits, etc.

Regularity compliance - Governments around the world are now looking at and implementing stricter employment and tax laws, especially regard contractors and remote working, which means EoR’s will need to recognise further compliance and have more local expertise.

Niche markets -Some players will specialise in certain markets, such as Technology, Healthcare, etc or large companies or maybe certain regions, rather than the one size fits all.

Enhanced data & Analytics - Clients will want better insights into local workforce costs, performance and compliance risks. EoR’s need to offer better local up to date information with further reporting and business intelligence tools.

Consolidation - It’s getting crowded in this market, expect acquisitions and mergers as it seems larger H.R. and financial platforms may absorb EoR’s or weaker players exit the market.

Flexible employment models - As differing work arrangements develop EoR’s will need to develop advanced tools to manage these mixed workforces of permanent, contract and temporary workers. 

Competitive Pressure on the market -Pricing has been under pressure and will likely decrease but may stabilise as the market matures, EoR’s will need to differentiate through service quality and technology rather than price alone.

It seems the EoR market will see steady growth, but players will need to adapt to survive, it will be interesting to see the next stage of development of this market and see who comes to the fore in the coming months and years. 
 
There is a definite need for EoR’s and clients should utilise the right one for their business, this market is changing and with it what it offers, so worth looking in detail to see the best use of EoR’s when growing your business worldwide. 

If you need any further information, contact me at grees@reesmarx.com.

Everyone's still arguing whether AI creates jobs or destroys them.

Watched Aaron Levie founder of Box on 20VC with Harry Stebbings, yesterday and he made a point that quietly reframes the whole debate:

Automation doesn't remove humans from the loop. It just exposes where the real bottleneck always was.

A hospital automates patient referrals. Great, you're off the phone. Except the appointment is still 18 months out, because the constraint was never the phone queue. It was doctor capacity.

Law firms are getting flooded with AI-drafted contract reviews from their own clients. More legal content than ever. But nothing moves faster, because the constraint was never drafting speed. It was the courts, and the humans who can actually file, defend, and approve.

So yes, the "humans in the loop" multiply. Not because AI is generous. Because every layer of automation surfaces the next constraint underneath, the one nobody could see while everything was slow.

If you're building or operating right now, that's the question worth sitting with: when your workflow gets 10x faster, what becomes the new bottleneck? That's where the next five years of value, and hiring, actually live.

What's the hidden bottleneck in your industry that automation is about to expose?

I recently ran a poll asking:

“𝐀𝐈 𝐢𝐬 𝐫𝐞𝐝𝐮𝐜𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐣𝐮𝐧𝐢𝐨𝐫 𝐡𝐢𝐫𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐢𝐧 𝐚𝐜𝐜𝐨𝐮𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐠 & 𝐥𝐚𝐰 𝐟𝐢𝐫𝐦𝐬. 𝐖𝐢𝐥𝐥 𝐭𝐡𝐢𝐬 𝐢𝐦𝐩𝐚𝐜𝐭 𝐟𝐮𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞 𝐩𝐚𝐫𝐭𝐧𝐞𝐫 𝐪𝐮𝐚𝐥𝐢𝐭𝐲?”

Here’s how people responded:
• 52% - Quality will decline
• 24% - Training pathways disrupted
• 17% - Quality will improve
• 7% - Unsure

At first glance, this looks like a concern about pipeline. 

But underneath, perhaps something deeper: the breakdown of the traditional apprenticeship model.

In both accounting and legal, junior years have historically been about high volume, low complexity work, document review, basic audit testing, drafting, reconciliation. Not glamorous, but critical. This is where pattern recognition, professional judgement, and risk intuition are built over time.

AI is now absorbing much of that layer:

In law, tools are handling document review, contract analysis, and due diligence at scale.

In accounting, automation is reducing manual audit work, reconciliations, and elements of tax preparation.

So, the issue isn’t just “fewer juniors.” It’s fewer opportunities to learn by doing.

That explains the 24% who see “training pathways disrupted.” They’re pointing to a structural shift: if entry level work disappears, where does judgement formation happen?

But the 17% who are optimistic aren’t wrong either.

AI doesn’t just remove work, it reshapes it. Juniors can now:
 • Interrogate outputs instead of producing first drafts
 • Focus earlier on exceptions, anomalies, and risk areas
 • Develop client-facing and advisory skills sooner

In theory, that could accelerate the path to high quality partners.

The catch? This only works if firms actively redesign development models.

Without intervention: Fewer reps, weaker intuition, risk to future partner quality.

With intentional redesign: Augmented learning, earlier exposure to complex thinking, potentially stronger, more strategic leaders.

The firms that win won’t be the ones that simply reduce junior hiring.
 
They’ll be the ones that rebuild the apprenticeship model for an AI first world.

Because partner quality has never just been about intelligence, it’s been about judgement.

And judgement is shaped through experience, not delegation.

3 projects every AI/ML portfolio needs before you apply Remote

A certificate doesn’t get you a remote AI job.

A portfolio does. Here's mine: https://lnkd.in/gTbsDuRx

I’ve been on both sides of this table.

When a hiring manager has 40 tabs open and ~90 seconds to decide, only a few things actually move the needle.

These 3 projects answer the only questions that matter:
1️⃣ Can you build something real with an LLM?
2️⃣ Can you keep it from breaking in production?
3️⃣ Do you understand what’s happening under the hood?

🔹 Project 1 — A RAG app that isn’t a toy

Every second post is a “first RAG chatbot”
(3 PDFs + defaults + basic UI)
That’s a tutorial. Not a portfolio.
A real RAG project has:
• A non-trivial corpus
• A chunking strategy you can defend
• An evaluation layer (even 20 queries is fine)
• A failure mode you identified and fixed

Example:
A Medical Assistant built on Davidson’s Principles of Medicine
→ Structured chunking (not random splits)
→ Constrained prompts
→ Real retrieval challenges
The chatbot is the easy part.

If you can clearly explain:
👉 why you chose your embeddings
👉 where retrieval failed

You’ve built Project 1.

🔹 Project 2 — Something that survives contact with reality

Everything works in a notebook.
Then reality shows up:
Latency. Cost. Rate limits. Bad inputs. Prompt injection.
Build something that handles it.

It doesn’t need to be fancy:
• API-backed model
• Containerized (Docker)
• Logging + input validation
• Fallback when things fail

Example:
SuperKart — retail forecasting API
→ Flask + Docker
→ Clean request/response contract
→ Built to run, not just demo

Nothing flashy.
But it answers the real question:
👉 Can you ship?
Bonus: Explain failure scenarios in your README.
That’s what experienced engineers look for.

🔹 Project 3 — Prove you understand the machinery

This is the separator. “I use AI” vs “I build AI”
Pick one:
• LoRA fine-tuning with before/after eval
• A reproducible eval harness
• A clean, readable transformer block from scratch

You don’t need to train the next GPT.
You just need to prove:
👉 the box isn’t black to you
⚙️ The quiet trio that makes all 3 land

Every project should include:
• README that respects time
(Problem → Architecture → What’s unique → How to run)
• Architecture diagram
(Boxes + arrows. Simple wins.)
• 90-sec demo
(Loom / GIF / screen recording)
If I can see it working in 90 seconds,
I’ll schedule the interview before I finish my coffee.
You can be a certificate collector…
or the person whose GitHub makes a recruiter stop scrolling.
Pick one.

💬 Drop your portfolio link — I’ll tell you which of the 3 you’re missing.

Your AI Hiring Stack Has a 103-Day Problem 

Last week a candidate posted in r/recruitinghell: "I got auto rejected by an AI chatbot five seconds after the interview. Most humiliating experience I've had."

3,400 upvotes. 268 comments. A screenshot attached.

That candidate doesn't know it yet, but on August 2, 2026, the system that rejected them becomes illegal to operate the way it just did.

103 days from now, every AI tool used in hiring inside the EU becomes a high-risk system under the EU AI Act.

Annual third-party bias audits. Full technical documentation. Human oversight for every rejected candidate. Transparency disclosures.

The penalty: up to €15M or 3% of global turnover, whichever hits harder. Regulators can also pull your AI hiring tools off the market. Mid-contract.

A five-second auto-rejection with no human review? That's a direct Article 14 violation.

If you use AI in hiring, here's what to do this week:

1. Ask your ATS vendor if they're classified as high-risk under Annex III. If they don't know, that's your answer.
2. Check whether you have outcome data disaggregated by gender, age, and ethnicity. Auditors need it.
3. Document your human review process for every rejected candidate.
4. Get an audit engagement letter signed. Certified auditors are booking up.

And if your company is based in the US, Canada, or India but posts remote roles open to EU applicants, you're in scope. The Act has extraterritorial reach.

The real constraint on AI adoption in 2026 is compliance infrastructure. Companies that build it now move fast later. Everyone else hits a wall in August.

EU AI Act portal → https://lnkd.in/e7pF_8sT

Stepping out of a 25-year corporate career into an innovation hub full of brilliant, young founders is an incredibly humbling experience. It strips your ego right back.

To scale Vancenco’s core Deep-Tech architectures alongside our live SaaS platforms, I have to be ruthlessly focused. I simply do not have the bandwidth to engage in 6-month enterprise sales cycles across multiple different industries.

Because my focus is shifting, I had to make an executive decision regarding the AI commercial intelligence and bidding platforms I originally built for the UK workwear and procurement sectors.

For the last 12 months, this architecture was locked behind a strict exclusivity agreement with two partners. That agreement has now ended.

Instead of renewing or hiring a sales team to sell this technology, I am going to give access away for free.

Having sat on both sides of the table building global supply chains and evaluating competitor bid submissions, I know exactly what wins contracts. While others have lost sight of it, for me at least, it has always been and will always remain... all in the detail.

Because processing this level of insight requires massive computational power, we have to spin up dedicated, isolated environments to guarantee zero-latency performance and data security.

I am handing this leverage back to the market to force a fairer, more equitable industry, allowing me to focus 100% on scaling our deep-tech core.

Outsourcing in 2026: cost‑saving or growth strategy?
 
Outsourcing goes beyond cost‑cutting — it’s a tool for flexibility and growth in uncertain times.
5 key benefits:
1. Cost reduction — pay for tasks, not fixed salaries; savings up to 1, 5 million RUB per month.
2. Time savings — the contractor handles coordination and HR issues.
3. Expertise access — professionals with refined skills (IT, accounting, logistics, etc.).
4. Flexibility — easily scale resources for seasonality or business goals.
5. Risk reduction — contractor manages compliance, documentation, and reporting.
2026 trends:
Remote work— hiring specialists regardless of location.
Niche specialization — demand for experts with unique skills.
Automation & AI — data processing, onboarding, time tracking, performance monitoring.
Global outsourcing market: 110 billion in 2024 → forecast 165 billion by 2033.
6 common outsourcing mistakes (and how to avoid them):
1. Obsession with low cost → compare value for money.
2. Ignoring industry experience → ask for relevant cases.
3. Neglecting reputation → check reviews and certifications.
4. Vague contract terms → define SLA, reporting, penalties.
5. Overlooking security → request ISO 27001, sign NDA.
6. Underestimating capacity → assess contractor’s resources and scalability.
 
💡 Conclusion: in 2026, outsourcing is a tool for instant scaling, expertise access, and tech adoption. Choosing the right partner makes your business more agile and competitive.

If your AI screening tool systematically penalises CVs with employment gaps, you may be indirectly discriminating against women returning from maternity leave, people with long-term disabilities, and specific ethnic demographics. That's the warning the ICO effectively made in March 2026.

Here's what quietly changed in the legal backdrop in 2026, and why HR teams need to pay attention:

• The Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 came fully into force on 5 February 2026. It replaced Article 22's blanket prohibition on automated decision-making with a legitimate-interests framework.

• The ICO's updated AI and Biometrics Strategy explicitly states they'll scrutinise the use of automated decision-making in recruitment in 2026, looking specifically at transparency, discrimination and redress.

• The ICO consultation on draft ADM guidance runs until 29 May 2026, with updated recruitment and selection guidance landing later this year.

• The Equality Act 2010 remains the sharpest risk. AI doesn't get a pass — if a tool produces a discriminatory outcome, the employer is liable.

The trap most SMBs walk into isn't malicious. It's this: you buy an AI screening tool, it works "well enough", and six months later you've made 80 hiring decisions with no documented human review, no Data Protection Impact Assessment, and no way to explain to a candidate why they were screened out.

A seven-point audit to run before the end of this quarter:

1. Map every AI touchpoint in your hiring funnel — including CV screening, video interview analysis, and chatbots
2. Run a DPIA before you deploy, not after an ICO letter arrives
3. Ask your vendor whose CVs the model was trained on
4. Define what "meaningful human review" actually looks like in your process
5. Write candidate-facing transparency notices
6. Test for adverse impact across protected characteristics
7. Set a retraining and monitoring cadence — at least quarterly

AI in recruitment is lawful in the UK. Careless AI in recruitment isn't. The difference is mostly documentation — and whether anyone has actually read the vendor contract.

Full 7-point audit checklist: https://lnkd.in/e6qGazYU

Outsourcing in 2026: cost‑saving or growth strategy?
 
Outsourcing goes beyond cost‑cutting — it’s a tool for flexibility and growth in uncertain times.
5 key benefits:
1. Cost reduction — pay for tasks, not fixed salaries; savings up to 1, 5 million RUB per month.
2. Time savings — the contractor handles coordination and HR issues.
3. Expertise access — professionals with refined skills (IT, accounting, logistics, etc.).
4. Flexibility — easily scale resources for seasonality or business goals.
5. Risk reduction — contractor manages compliance, documentation, and reporting.
2026 trends:
Remote work— hiring specialists regardless of location.
Niche specialization — demand for experts with unique skills.
Automation & AI — data processing, onboarding, time tracking, performance monitoring.
Global outsourcing market: 110 billion in 2024 → forecast 165 billion by 2033.
6 common outsourcing mistakes (and how to avoid them):
1. Obsession with low cost → compare value for money.
2. Ignoring industry experience → ask for relevant cases.
3. Neglecting reputation → check reviews and certifications.
4. Vague contract terms → define SLA, reporting, penalties.
5. Overlooking security → request ISO 27001, sign NDA.
6. Underestimating capacity → assess contractor’s resources and scalability.
 
💡 Conclusion: in 2026, outsourcing is a tool for instant scaling, expertise access, and tech adoption. Choosing the right partner makes your business more agile and competitive.

There’s a ceiling most perm tech recruiters don’t realise they’ve hit.

You can be consistent. You can bill well.

But if you’re sat on a perm-only desk, continuing in the same old school tech market, then you’re playing in a narrower lane.

The shift is already happening in Data & AI.

Hiring isn’t just about filling roles. It’s about advising businesses on how to become AI-ready, what capability they actually need, and how to structure teams that didn’t even exist a few years ago.

That’s where the real billing potential sits.

I met with a client who have fully leaned into this. Not just talking about AI, actually embedding themselves in the market.

They’re partnering with PE-backed businesses, working on leadership hires like Heads of AI and Chief Data Officers, while also delivering on the engineers and specialist roles.

But the interesting bit…

They operate a true dual desk model.

Same clients. Same projects. Multiple revenue streams from the same relationship.

On top of that, they invest heavily in building communities. Events, roundtables, face to face engagement. Not just BD calls, actual market presence. The kind that brings clients to you.

If you’re a strong perm tech recruiter, you already know how to win business and deliver.

The question is, what happens when you plug that skillset into a model that gives you; access to both perm and contract revenue, clients operating at the forefront of Data and AI, and a brand that’s already trusted.

If you feel like you’re working hard but there’s another level you’re not quite reaching, this could be the place where it all clicks.

Message me if you’re curious...

An Indian startup just raised 66 crore rupees to do background checks.

Background verification is the most boring, commoditized, low-margin HR service on the planet. So why are top-tier VCs pouring millions into it?

The startup is Delhi-based TrackCheck.

If they were just hiring humans to call your old boss and verify your degrees, no VC in the world would touch them. That is a services business with thin margins and no leverage.

But TrackCheck is not that.

They are building an autonomous AI recruiting agent. They are taking a slow, manual, weeks-long process and turning it into a high-speed instant API call for massive enterprises.

Same output. Completely different architecture. Completely different margin structure.

Here is the VC thesis nobody on Twitter is talking about. It is called Unsexy AI.

Everyone is busy building the next cool consumer chatbot. Meanwhile institutional capital is quietly funding startups that automate the boring, bloated, high-volume middle-management tasks of corporate India.

→ Background verification
→ Compliance documentation
→ Invoice reconciliation
→ KYC for enterprises
→ Procurement workflows
→ Legal document review
→ Internal audit processes

These are not glamorous. You will not see them in a Product Hunt launch post. They will never go viral on Twitter.

But they are massive markets hiding in plain sight. They have real budgets, real contracts, and real enterprise customers ready to pay serious money the moment you offer 10x better economics.

The pattern is clear:

→ Take a slow manual service that every enterprise already buys
→ Replace the humans with AI agents running at scale
→ Turn it from a services business into a software platform
→ Charge software margins on what used to be services revenue

Services companies trade at 1 to 2x revenue. Software companies trade at 10 to 20x revenue.

AI is the conversion lever between those two worlds.

If you can turn a slow back office process into a high-margin software platform, VCs will write you a blank check. Not because the idea is sexy. Because the financial transformation is enormous.

The real builders of 2026 are not building the next consumer AI app. They are quietly automating the boring internal workflows of the Fortune 500 one vertical at a time.

Consumer AI is where attention goes. Enterprise unsexy AI is where the money goes.

What is the most boring corporate process you would automate with AI tomorrow if you had the chance?

415,000 Open Jobs and a Retirement Wave: How AI Audio Screening Is Solving Manufacturing's Toughest Hiring Challenge

U.S. manufacturing doesn't just have a hiring problem. It has a math problem.

415,000 open roles today. 26% of the workforce nearing retirement by 2030. A reshoring boom demanding more domestic production capacity. And a Gen Z workforce that quits within a year if there's no clear advancement path.

Deloitte's projection: 1.9 million manufacturing positions could remain unfilled by 2033 without serious intervention.

Traditional screening was built for a different era. A recruiter running phone screens can't keep pace with a plant that needs 50–100 frontline hires in three weeks. Every day a candidate waits, a competitor makes an offer.

AI audio screening changes the arithmetic. OVI's structured audio chats evaluate candidates on the practical factors that actually matter for frontline fit — salary expectations, relocation, certifications, culture fit — without scheduling, without video, without biometric analysis.

The compliance piece matters here too: transcript-only analysis, human-in-the-loop decisions, and alignment with NYC LL144, GDPR, and EU AI Act readiness. For manufacturers running government contracts under reshoring programs, auditability isn't a nice-to-have.

Starting at $99/month, this is now accessible to mid-market manufacturers — not just enterprise players with six-figure TA budgets.

The plants that move first will fill their lines first.

​The "AI Cheat Code" failed. 

Here’s why your tech hiring is still taking 50% longer.

​I’ve been catching up on the feedback from the CHRO South Africa Summit held here in Cape Town earlier this month, and the sentiment is striking. While last year was dominated by the hype of AI being the "ultimate shortcut" for recruitment, the reality has been the opposite.

​Instead of speed, it created a bottleneck.

​The Feedback from the Top:
The summit highlighted a growing sense of fear, anxiety, and a serious warning against over-reliance on AI tools. What was meant to simplify things has actually confused decision-makers, leading to a "wait and see" culture.
​I’m seeing this first-hand. Placements are dipping, and I’ve chatted with fellow veteran recruiters who notice companies sitting on roles for an entire quarter before pulling the trigger.

​The Pivot: From Tools to Accountability
The big trend now? Moving away from tool-dependency and getting back to Strategic Leadership.
​This is why we are seeing a massive shift toward embedding technical recruiters directly into teams on a contract basis. It doesn’t matter if you are looking for a Junior dev or Executive leadership—tech hiring isn't a volume game; it’s a precision game.

​Companies are realizing they don't need a "CV pusher" or an algorithm. They need a specialist who understands the nuance of the stack and the business objectives.

​The Solution:
If your time-to-hire is lagging, it’s likely because your process lacks technical empathy. This is where a trusted agency partner or a specialist TA comes in—to provide the expert support that a tool simply can't replicate. You need someone who can navigate the local market, manage the anxiety, and deliver accountability.

​Stop waiting for a "cheat code." Let’s get back to human-led, strategic placement.

Series A funded. No GC. It happens more than investors would like to admit.

The median pre-money Series A valuation reached $49.3 million in Q3 2025, the highest on record. At that level of investment, investors expect a mature and legally defensible business. Most Series A founders know this. And yet, a significant number arrive at that fundraise - and through it - without a single in-house lawyer.

The reasons are understandable. Legal feels like overhead before product-market fit. Outside counsel covers the acute moments. The founding team is stretched. But the pattern has a cost, and that cost compounds.

Investors will scrutinise your entire legal foundation through a virtual data room: corporate records, IP documentation, material contracts, employment agreements, and governance. Incomplete documentation is a major red flag. With the median time between Seed and Series A now sitting at 26 months, that's 26 months during which legal housekeeping either happens continuously or gets rebuilt from scratch under deal pressure. 

Rebuilding it under deal pressure is expensive. Due diligence alone typically runs 0.5 to 2% of deal value, and a disorganised data room forces advisers to redo analyses when new files appear late; adding cost and eroding deal momentum. For a $49 million round, that's a material number. And that's before accounting for the founder and leadership time lost to document retrieval and fire-fighting.

If you hit a headcount of 50 people and you don't yet have a lawyer on the team, it might be worth thinking about kickstarting a hiring process. Most Series A companies are well past that threshold. The general counsel isn't just a hire for the fundraise; they're the person who makes the next fundraise cleaner, the commercial contracts faster, and the governance credible to a board that now includes institutional investors.

In 2026, legal hiring is being shaped by the ability to manage risk, navigate regulatory complexity, and embed governance into business operations; not as a cost centre, but as a strategic function.

The founders who treat their first legal hire as a strategic decision, not an administrative one, tend to find out why at Series B.

This is what The Talent Signal covers every week. Hiring data, market gaps, and what it means for founders, investors, and the people building careers in AI and Web3.

- Tabor Labs Advisory

Sources: Allied Venture Partners Series A Guide 2026 · HSBC Innovation Banking Legal Hire Guide · Peony Due Diligence Costs 2026 · The National Law Review Legal Hiring 2026 · Qubit Capital Due Diligence 2026

5 events that tell you a company is about to hire. None of them appear on job boards.

1. Funding announcement. Any round above Series A. They will hire. The question is when, how many, and whether you get there first.

2. Leadership hire. New CEO, CTO, CPO, or VP. New leaders build teams around people they trust. Roles open before the organisation chart is updated.

3. Contract win. Government contracts. Enterprise deals. Partnership announcements. More work means more headcount. Every time.

4. Innovation award or certification. ISO certification. AI implementation investment. New accreditation. Companies investing in capability are building the team to deliver it.

5. Market expansion. New office. New geography. New product vertical. Expansion always precedes the job ad.

Any one of these means hiring is coming. Specialists who act on them early win the mandate before anyone else knows it exists.

These are the pre-hire indicators Hirelytiq identifies every day, filtered to your exact sector, ranked by probability.
